one_line_takeaway: A tailored GRPO reward (modular formatting/correctness/reasoning scores with dynamic anti-reward-hacking weighting) lets small general-purpose LLMs reason their way to more balanced, distribution-robust vulnerability detection, beating SFT by up to 29 macro-F1 points chiefly by rescuing the Not-Vulnerable class.
key_finding: GRPO consistently beats both zero-shot baselines and SFT across all model/dataset combinations. Against SFT it improves macro F1 by 1-29 points and accuracy by 4-17 points, with most gains coming from rescuing the Not-Vulnerable class that SFT collapses (e.g., Qwen 2.5 on DiverseVul Not-Vulnerable recall rises from 0.07 under SFT to 0.61 under GRPO). Because gains persist on out-of-distribution datasets (BigVul, CleanVul) and unseen programming languages (Java macro F1 0.25->0.46, Python 0.34->0.58 for Qwen), the authors attribute the improvement to GRPO-encouraged reasoning rather than mere fine-tuning. A naive static reward (0.5*F + 0.5*C) triggers reward hacking -- both LLaMA 8B and Qwen 2.5 label everything Vulnerable (macro F1 0.33) -- which the dynamic reward fixes. GRPO also produces shorter, more focused reasoning (LLaMA 8B on DiverseVul ~600 tokens base/SFT vs ~340 GRPO) and explanations that align better with the correct CWE definitions.
core_contribution: The paper adapts Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a DeepSeek-style RL fine-tuning method, to the task of software vulnerability detection with small, general-purpose (non-code-specialized) instruction-tuned LLMs. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a task-specific modular reward function scoring model outputs along three axes -- Formatting, Correctness, and Reasoning -- combined with (2) a Dynamic Reward Module that adaptively reweights formatting vs. correctness sub-rewards over training (coefficient alpha shifting from tau_A=0.9 toward tau_B=0.2 once formatting is learned) plus power-scaling and softmax to prevent reward hacking (models trivially labeling everything 'vulnerable'). The authors validate this via three research questions: RQ1 (zero-shot reasoning ability of small LLMs), RQ2 (whether GRPO-trained self-reasoning improves detection), and RQ3 (how GRPO differs from standard SFT). Note: the DNN detectors (DeepVulSeeker, VUDENC, TrVD, etc.), RAG methods (Vul-RAG), and prompting methods (ProRLearn) discussed are cited Related Work in Section 7, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is a defensive detection-capability paper, not an attack or adversarial-ML paper. There is no adversary or attack model. The security setting is binary classification of source-code functions as Vulnerable vs. Not Vulnerable (Vulnerable = positive class), aimed at assisting developers/auditors in finding exploitable weaknesses (buffer overflows, injections, memory-safety errors, etc.). The paper's key concern that is threat-adjacent is generalization to previously unseen vulnerabilities/codebases and the trustworthiness/explainability of model reasoning for security-critical decisions. No injected adversarial inputs, evasion, or poisoning are studied.
limitations: Only small models (3B-8B) are studied; results may not transfer to larger models. Training is on a single C-language dataset (DiverseVul), and CleanVul construction is LLM-heuristic-based (90.6% correctness, no CWE labels), so label noise is a factor. The task is coarse binary function-level classification, not localization or CWE typing. GRPO does not uniformly help: LLaMA 8B shows little to no per-CWE gain on the in-distribution DiverseVul split (SFT can memorize patterns better there), and LLaMA 3B regresses on some CWEs (e.g., CWE-20, CWE-190) and on CWE-125 reasoning alignment. The beta ablation shows a genuine tradeoff (higher beta lowers false negatives... actually raises TN but also FN), so hyperparameters materially shift the risk profile. Several table cross-references in the text are inconsistent (e.g., Section 5 compares 'Table 6 and Table 1' where Table 4 is meant), and the Table 10 DiverseVul percentages appear to duplicate Not-Vulnerable and Vulnerable rows.

datasets_used: DiverseVul (18,945 vulnerable functions across 150 CWEs + 330,492 non-vulnerable functions; C code; 80/20 train/test split, used as in-distribution)
BigVul (large-scale C/C++ CVE-crawled dataset; used as out-of-distribution test)

CleanVul (8,203 functions across 6 languages: Java, Python, JavaScript, C#, C, C++; LLM-heuristic pipeline with 90.6% overall correctness, no per-CWE labels; out-of-distribution / cross-language test)
2024 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses (subset of ~10-15 CWEs, e.g. CWE-787, CWE-119, CWE-20, CWE-125, CWE-416, CWE-476, CWE-190, CWE-78, used for per-CWE analysis)
quantitative_results: Zero-shot best macro F1 (Qwen 2.5, no-reasoning): CleanVul 0.57, DiverseVul 0.52, BigVul 0.54
GRPO vs SFT overall: macro F1 improves by 1-29 points; accuracy improves by 4-17 points
Qwen 2.5 DiverseVul (in-distribution) GRPO: accuracy 0.67, macro F1 0.67 (vs best zero-shot baseline 0.52)
Not-Vulnerable recall (DiverseVul, Qwen 2.5): 0.07 under SFT -> 0.61 under GRPO
Not-Vulnerable recall (CleanVul, LLaMA 8B): 0.09 under SFT -> 0.42 under GRPO
SFT bias: Not-Vulnerable recall never exceeds 0.24 and class F1 peaks at 0.37 (Qwen 2.5 / LLaMA 3B); LLaMA 8B DiverseVul SFT Not-Vulnerable recall 0.43, F1 0.58
Static-reward reward hacking (DiverseVul): LLaMA 8B and Qwen 2.5 collapse to all-Vulnerable, macro F1 0.33 (Not-Vulnerable P/R/F1 all 0.00)
Cross-language macro F1 gains (GRPO vs SFT baseline): Qwen 2.5 Java 0.25->0.46, Python 0.34->0.58; consistent gains for LLaMA 3B/8B on Python and Java
Reasoning length (correct predictions, DiverseVul): LLaMA 8B ~600 tokens (Base/SFT) -> ~340 tokens (GRPO)
BigVul GRPO example (LLaMA 8B): Vulnerable recall jumps from 0.06 (best zero-shot) to 0.91; overall accuracy 0.62
Beta (KL) ablation: beta=1e-4 raises True-Negative rate but also False-Negative rate (more conservative); beta=1e-6 keeps FN low (more risk-taking, favors catching vulnerabilities)
baselines_compared: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on binary ground-truth labels with no-reasoning prompt
Static/naive summed reward variant (r = 0.5*F + 0.5*C) as an internal ablation baseline against the dynamic reward
